skill.name: azure-security-keyvault-keys-java
skill.description: Guides Java developers through Azure Key Vault Keys SDK tasks for creating, managing, rotating, importing, backing up, and using cryptographic keys.
skill.summary: Java reference for Azure Key Vault key management and cryptographic operations.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static external-command findings are false positives caused by Markdown code fences around Java, XML, and Bash examples. The skill is not malicious, but it documents sensitive key backup, raw key import, permanent purge, and a legacy RSA1_5 algorithm, so publication should include a security warning.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive key backup is written to a local file
medium_findings.description: The backup example stores Key Vault backup bytes in key-backup.blob and reads them back from disk. This is legitimate SDK usage, but the backup blob is sensitive material and could be exposed by local storage, logs, or source control.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file shows direct backup bytes written with Files.write and read with Files.readAllBytes. The behavior is an example, not hidden code execution, so the risk is contextual rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Permanent key purge example can destroy recoverable keys
medium_findings.description: The delete section includes purgeDeletedKey after starting deletion. This is an authorized administrative operation, but users could permanently remove keys if they copy the example without safeguards.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The purge call is explicit and has permanent security impact. It appears in documentation context, which lowers malicious confidence but not operational risk.

medium_findings.title: Legacy RSA1_5 encryption algorithm is listed without warning
medium_findings.description: The encryption algorithm table lists RSA1_5 alongside OAEP choices. The examples use RSA_OAEP, but the table does not warn readers to avoid RSA1_5 except for legacy compatibility.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static weak-crypto hit is confirmed by the RSA1_5 entry. The surrounding content recommends RSA_OAEP in examples, so this is a guidance issue rather than clear insecure implementation.

medium_findings.title: Raw private key material import example needs stronger handling guidance
medium_findings.description: The import example constructs a JsonWebKey with private RSA components such as a private exponent. Importing existing key material is valid, but the skill does not add warnings about handling, storage, or cleanup of private components.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The example explicitly references privateExponent and imports key material. It is legitimate SDK documentation, but it involves highly sensitive values.

low_findings.title: Static external-command detections are Markdown fences
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick findings correspond to Markdown code fences for XML, Java, and Bash examples. No evidence found of executable shell commands or dynamic command construction in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The detected backticks are standard Markdown delimiters. Review of SKILL.md found documentation snippets, not Ruby backtick execution or shell invocation logic.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs are Azure Key Vault placeholders
low_findings.description: The network findings point to placeholder Azure Key Vault URLs and an environment variable example. These are expected for an Azure SDK reference, but users should replace them with tenant-owned vault endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs use <vault-name> placeholders and point to the standard vault.azure.net service. No evidence found of exfiltration endpoints or unauthorized network destinations.

low_findings.title: Certificate and key-file detections are terminology matches
low_findings.description: Several sensitive-file detections are triggered by Azure Key Vault class names, key identifiers, and key management examples. No embedded private key, certificate file, or hardcoded secret value was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content is SDK terminology and placeholder key identifiers. The only confirmed sensitive handling is documented separately for backup and raw import examples.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local persistence of key backup material
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill shows writing a Key Vault backup blob to local storage. Backup files should be encrypted, access controlled, and excluded from source control.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code example directly writes backup bytes to a named file. This is visible documentation, but the data class is sensitive.

dangerous_patterns.title: Administrative key purge operation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes an example that purges a deleted key. Purge should require explicit authorization and operational confirmation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The call is explicit and destructive. It is not concealed, so the risk is misuse rather than malicious behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Private key component handling
dangerous_patterns.description: The import example references private RSA components before sending them to Key Vault. Users need strict memory, logging, and storage controls for these values.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The example includes privateExponent in a JsonWebKey. The skill does not include a secret value, but the workflow handles private material.

content.user_title: Build Azure Key Vault Keys Java Workflows
content.value_statement: Java teams need reliable patterns for Azure Key Vault key management and cryptographic operations. This skill provides focused SDK examples for keys, crypto clients, rotation, backup, import, and error handling.
actual_capabilities: Shows Maven dependency setup for azure-security-keyvault-keys.
Demonstrates synchronous and asynchronous KeyClient creation.
Covers RSA, EC, OCT, and HSM-backed key creation options.
Provides examples for encrypt, decrypt, sign, verify, wrap, and unwrap operations.
Explains key deletion, recovery, backup, restore, rotation, and import flows.
Lists supported encryption and signature algorithm names.
limitations: Does not validate Azure tenant configuration or Key Vault access policies.
Does not provide runnable project scaffolding or tests.
Does not include complete private key import variables or secure storage controls.
Uses SDK version 4.9.0 and may need updates for newer releases.

use_cases.title: Add Key Vault keys to a Java service
use_cases.description: Use the client setup and key creation examples to add managed keys to a backend service.

use_cases.title: Plan cryptographic operations with Managed HSM
use_cases.description: Compare key types, HSM options, and crypto operations before implementing production workflows.

use_cases.title: Review key lifecycle procedures
use_cases.description: Use the rotation, backup, restore, delete, and recover examples to document operational runbooks.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Key Vault client
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a Java project
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to show how to configure the Azure Key Vault Keys Java dependency and create a KeyClient with DefaultAzureCredential.

prompt_templates.title: Generate managed keys
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to draft Java examples for creating RSA, EC, and HSM-backed keys with expiration, enabled state, operations, and tags.

prompt_templates.title: Implement cryptographic operations
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to explain how a Java service can encrypt, decrypt, sign, verify, wrap, and unwrap keys through CryptographyClient.

prompt_templates.title: Design lifecycle controls
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to create a Key Vault key lifecycle plan covering rotation policy, backup, restore, recovery, purge safeguards, and import risks.

output_examples.input: Ask for Java client setup for Azure Key Vault Keys.
output_examples.output: A concise setup guide with the Maven dependency, KeyClientBuilder usage, vault URL placement, and DefaultAzureCredential notes.

output_examples.input: Ask how to perform encryption and signing with a stored key.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step explanation of CryptographyClient usage, algorithm selection, digest creation, and result handling.

output_examples.input: Ask for a key lifecycle checklist.
output: Set rotation policies before production use.
Protect backup blobs as sensitive material.

best_practices: Use least-privilege Azure identities and separate keys by operation or application boundary.
Prefer OAEP or PSS algorithms unless a legacy system requires another option.
Protect backup blobs and imported key material with strict storage and access controls.
anti_patterns: Copying purge examples into automation without approval gates.
Storing key backup blobs in source control, shared folders, or unencrypted disks.
Using legacy RSA1_5 encryption for new systems without a documented compatibility reason.

faq.question: Does this skill execute Azure commands?
faq.answer: No. It is a Markdown reference with Java SDK examples and does not execute commands by itself.

faq.question: Which Azure SDK package does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers com.azure:azure-security-keyvault-keys for Java Key Vault Keys workflows.

faq.question: Can it help with Managed HSM keys?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes examples for HSM-backed RSA, EC, and OCT keys where supported.

faq.question: Does it include secure production defaults?
faq.answer: Partly. It recommends HSM keys, soft delete, rotation, and least privilege, but users must add local security controls.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk medium?
faq.answer: The content handles sensitive key workflows, local backups, raw imports, purge operations, and a legacy algorithm listing.

faq.question: Should users copy every example directly?
faq.answer: No. Users should adapt examples to their vault policies, identity model, approval process, and secret handling rules.
